perturbation.original_text: 3.1.4 keep the Confidential Information in a safe and secure place and establish and maintain adequate security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use, including but not limited to implementing any reasonable security measures proposed by the Discloser from time to time;
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1.4 keep the Confidential Information in a reasonably safe place and consider implementing security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from occasional unauthorized access or use, potentially taking into account some security measures suggested by the Discloser on occasion;
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'safe and secure place' with 'reasonably safe place', 'adequate security measures' with 'consider implementing security measures', 'unauthorised access or use' with 'occasional unauthorized access', and 'implementing any reasonable security measures proposed by the Discloser from time to time' with 'potentially taking into account some security measures suggested by the Discloser on occasion'. These changes weaken the obligation to protect confidential information and create uncertainty about the required level of security.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of GDPR mandates appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including, inter alia, as appropriate: (a) the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; (b) the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; (c) the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; (d) a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. The changes made in the modified text do not ensure the same level of security.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts GDPR Article 32, as it weakens the security measures required to protect personal data. The original text mandates 'adequate security measures,' while the changed text suggests 'consider implementing security measures,' significantly reducing the obligation. Snippet 2 from GDPR Art. 32 explicitly states that controllers and processors 'shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk'. The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to safeguard confidential information, failing to ensure the same level of security required by GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.3 procures that the Representative shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed, comply with this agreement as if it were the Recipient; and
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.3 encourages the Representative to, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed, consider this agreement as a general guideline; and
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'procures that the Representative shall comply with this agreement' with 'encourages the Representative to consider this agreement as a general guideline' weakens the obligation. Compliance becomes voluntary and the agreement serves only as a suggestion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Belgian Code of Economic Law - Book XI, Title 3, Chapter 1 (Trade Secrets)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Belgian Code of Economic Law protects trade secrets and requires that anyone receiving confidential information takes adequate measures to maintain its confidentiality. The modification weakens the requirement to maintain confidentiality, so it contradicts the need for adequate measures to protect the trade secret. Article XI.331/1 of the Belgian Code of Economic Law outlines the legal framework for the protection of trade secrets and the responsibilities of those handling such information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute A statute is a law enacted by a legislature. Statutes are also called acts, such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 or the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . Federal laws must be passed by both houses of Congress, the House of Representative and the Senate, and then usually require approval from the President before they can take effect. As explained by the Library of Congress , enacted federal statutes are published multiple times. First, each individual law is published as a “slip law.” Then, all of the slip laws for each session of Congress are published together as “session laws.” Finally, all laws that are of a “general and permanent nature” are eventually compiled into the United States Code , and also the Revised Statutes of the United States. State statutes can be found through the LII's page on Constitutions, Statutes, and Codes . Readers can also attempt to find state statutes organized by topic: Topical Index: State Statutes . [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS wex definitions government legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with the agreement by the Representative regarding Confidential Information, treating them as the Recipient. The changed text weakens this obligation to a mere suggestion ('encourages...to consider this agreement as a general guideline'). This contradicts the Law Explanation's assertion that trade secrets require adequate measures to maintain confidentiality, as the modified clause does not ensure such measures, effectively making compliance voluntary rather than obligatory.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 The parties each acknowledge that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, each party shall be entitled to seek the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 The parties acknowledge that damages may not always be an adequate remedy for a breach of this agreement. Therefore, each party may consider seeking equitable relief, such as injunctive relief, for a potential breach of this agreement, but is not obligated to do so.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the agreement. By replacing 'damages alone would not be an adequate remedy' with 'damages may not always be an adequate remedy' and 'each party shall be entitled to seek' with 'each party may consider seeking, but is not obligated to do so', the provision regarding equitable relief becomes discretionary and uncertain.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1134 (Old Civil Code - now Article 5.64 New Civil Code)
perturbation.law_explanation: According to article 1134 (old civil code - now article 5.64 of the new civil code) of the Belgian Civil Code, agreements lawfully entered into have the force of law for those who have made them. By making the remedy discretionary, the modified clause undermines the legally binding nature of the agreement, so it is less enforced. The original text grants each party the right to seek equitable relief, while the modified text merely allows parties to consider such action without obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes

Code , and Canadian jurisprudence in both Québec and the common law provinces pertaining to contractual and pre-contractual negotiation v t e In contract law , the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is a general presumption that the parties to a contract will deal with each other honestly, fairly, and in good faith , so as to not destroy the right of the other party or parties to receive the benefits of the contract. It is implied in a number of contract types in order to reinforce the express covenants or promises of the contract. A lawsuit (or a cause of action ) based upon the breach of the covenant may arise when one party to the contract attempts to claim the benefit of a technical excuse for breaching the contract, or when he or she uses specific contractual terms in isolation in order to refuse to perform his or her contractual obligations, despite the general circumstances and understandings between the parties. When a court or trier of fact interprets a contract, there is always an "implied covenant of good fai
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates equitable relief for breaches, while the changed text makes it discretionary. The Law Explanation points out this undermines the legally binding nature of the agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, suggests that parties should not act to destroy the benefits of the contract for the other party. By changing 'shall be entitled to seek' to 'may consider seeking, but is not obligated to do so,' the modified text weakens the enforceability and creates ambiguity, thereby contradicting the expectation of good faith and potentially denying a party the full benefit of the original agreement.
